meta.title: A New Skyline
meta.author: Susan Lennox
meta.focusQuestion: How has New York City's skyline changed?

sections.title: Introduction
paragraphs: What would Paris be without the Eiffel Tower, or London without its famous clock tower, Big Ben? <s>Iconic structures such as these, along with surrounding buildings</s> <v>create</v> <o>an outline</o> against the sky, called a skyline. <s>Skylines</s> <v>are</v> <o>unique identifiers</o> of places, just as your fingerprints are one-of-a-kind identifiers of you. Unlike fingerprints, however, <s>skylines</s> <v>don't stay</v> <o>the same</o> forever. They grow and change, sometimes gradually and at other times quite suddenly.

<s>New York City</s> <v>is</v> <o>a great example</o> of a place whose skyline has experienced both slow and rapid change. In recent years, New York's skyline has been altered dramatically. Buildings and structures have risen, fallen, and risen again. New York's skyline has continuously evolved.

sections.title: Growing the Big Apple
paragraphs: During the nineteenth century, <s>New York City's famous skyline</s>, along with its diverse population, <v>began to grow</v> rapidly. With the end of the Civil War came freed slaves from the South in search of work. Later, <s>waves of Europeans</s> <v>immigrated</v> <o>to the United States</o> through the port of New York. Many of them stayed on New York's Manhattan Island. By the late 1800s, New York was the most densely populated city in the country; by 1925, it was the most densely populated city in the world.

All the city's new residents needed places to live, work, and shop. Gradually, <s>the city</s> <v>expanded</v> <o>northward</o> as more stores, factories, and homes were built. However, <s>Manhattan Island</s> <v>is</v> <o>only about 23 square miles</o> in area. There was no more room for the city to expand outward; the only place to grow was up.

To do that, <s>new building methods</s> <v>had to be employed</v>. Up until that time, <s>walls</s> <v>had been used</v> <o>to support a building's weight</o>. The walls on a building's ground floor had to be strong enough to bear the weight of all the floors and the roof above them. This type of construction limited a building's height. There was no practical way to make walls thick and strong enough to support a building with dozens of floors.

A new way of building involved using a steel frame instead of walls to support the structure. <s>Gigantic steel beams</s> <v>were connected</v> to form <o>the building's skeleton</o>. Then the walls were hung from the beams, much like curtains from a rod, and attached at the bottom to the floor. With the use of steel frame construction, the walls of the building's upper floors were not weighing heavily on the lower floors' walls. The weight of the walls was distributed along the steel beams holding them up.

<s>Steel frame construction</s> <v>made</v> <o>it possible</o> for buildings to reach new heights. By the turn of the twentieth century, New York City housed a number of huge structures that came to be called skyscrapers. <s>Skyscrapers</s> <v>were going up</v> one after another as <s>builders and architects</s> <v>competed</v> <o>to make the world's tallest building</o>.

sections.title: Building an Empire
paragraphs: By the late 1920s, New York City's skyline had risen to new heights. It was during this time that two of the city's best-known structures were built.

In 1928, the builder of the seventy-seven-story Chrysler Building wanted to make it the world's tallest. So fierce was the competition for this title that the Chrysler builder smuggled a tall spire onto the top of his structure. The secret plan worked: in 1930, the Chrysler Building became not only the world's tallest structure but also the only one to stand taller than 1,000 feet (305 m).

The Chrysler Building's reign would not last. Just blocks away, <s>a steel-framed behemoth</s> <v>was rising</v>. Not only would the Empire State Building take the crown for world's tallest, but it would also come to define the city it called home.

<s>Workers</s> <v>broke ground</v> <o>on the Empire State Building</o> in January 1930. Amazingly, it was completed just 410 days later. The steel and granite building had 103 floors and stood over 1,400 feet (427 m) tall. More than 3,000 workers contributed skills and labor to finish the building.

The completed building had 64 working elevators that took less than one minute to get to the 80th floor. <s>An outside observation deck on the 86th floor and an inside deck on the 102nd floor</s> <v>provided</v> <o>panoramic views of the city</o>.

<s>The Empire State Building</s> <v>symbolized</v> <o>New York City</o>. It was named one of the Seven Wonders of the Modern World and was featured in many books and movies. It wore the crown of world's tallest building for nearly forty years until deposed by twin giants rising in the lower part of Manhattan.

sections.title: Twin Giants Rise, Then Fall
paragraphs: After the Empire State Building was completed, <s>skyscraper construction</s> <v>slowed down</v>. Mammoth structures were costly to build, and the American economy was struggling. It was not until 1970 that the city's skyline would grow even higher.

In the 1960s, <s>city officials</s> <v>considered building</v> <o>a "world trade center"</o>—a hub for international commerce—in Lower Manhattan. <s>The Port Authority of New York and New Jersey</s> <v>was</v> <o>in charge of the project</o>. <s>Architect Minoru Yamasaki</s> <v>designed</v> <o>the buildings</o>. <s>He</s> <v>proposed</v> <o>twin towers</o> as part of the multibuilding complex of offices and storefronts. <s>The 110-story towers</s> <v>would include</v> <o>some structural innovations</o>.

<s>One innovation</s> <v>was</v> <o>the use of tube-frame construction</o> instead of steel-frame construction. <s>Each tower</s> <v>was</v> <o>similar to a hollow square pipe</o>. <s>The outside perimeter of the building</s> <v>had</v> <o>columns</o> that were spaced close together. Surrounding the edge of the building with main support columns created more usable floor space inside.

<s>Another innovation</s> <v>was</v> <o>the use of sky lobbies</o>. The taller a building is, the more elevators it needs. Yamasaki proposed an elevator system modeled after the city's subway system. The towers had both express and local elevators.

People could take an express elevator to a sky lobby on the forty-fourth floor that would bypass the first forty-three floors. Then they would transfer to a local elevator, which would stop at every floor, to get to their office on the forty-eighth floor. <s>This system</s> <v>was</v> <o>a new, efficient way</o> to transport people vertically, and it reduced the number of elevator shafts needed.

<s>Construction of the towers</s> <v>began</v> in 1966. <s>The North Tower</s> <v>was completed</v> in December 1970, topping out at 1,727 feet (526 m) at the top of its spire. A restaurant called Windows on the World offered diners a breathtaking view from the top of the North Tower. The South Tower, reaching 1,362 feet (415 m) in height, was finished seven months later. Beneath both buildings was a parking garage that could accommodate up to two thousand vehicles. Railroad and subway stations were situated underneath the parking garage.

<s>The Twin Towers</s> <v>were</v> <o>a striking addition</o> to the city's skyline. Because they were built away from the city's other tall skyscrapers, <s>they</s> <v>dwarfed</v> <o>the surrounding buildings</o> and <v>were</v> <o>the most prominent structures on Manhattan Island</o>. For thirty years, the World Trade Center towers were the tallest buildings in New York. Then disaster struck.

On the morning of September 11, 2001, <s>hijackers</s> <v>flew</v> <o>two passenger jets</o> into the towers. Within an hour after the attack, <s>the South Tower</s> <v>began to fall</v> in on itself and crumble to the ground. The North Tower followed thirty minutes later. New Yorkers watched in stunned disbelief as these enormous structures were reduced to heaps of burning rubble. Nearly three thousand people died as a result of the attack.

sections.title: A New Tower Rises from the Ashes
paragraphs: <s>The World Trade Center tragedy</s> <v>devastated</v> <o>New York and the rest of the country</o>. <s>New Yorkers</s> <v>mourned</v> <o>the loss of their neighbors and their landmark</o>. At the same time, they vowed to rebuild at the World Trade Center site.

Excavating the ruins of the old towers took time. Plans for the site took years to develop; there were many opinions on what should be done, and many designs were submitted with considerable controversy. Finally, the Port Authority unveiled the design for the new World Trade Center complex. One area was set aside as a memorial park for those who lost their lives that day. The sites where the original buildings stood are now reflecting pools with cascading waterfalls. A memorial museum contains items from the old towers.

Nearby, a new 104-story tower has been constructed. Nicknamed the Freedom Tower, <s>the structure</s> <v>rises</v> <o>an astonishing 1,776 feet</o> (541 m). <s>The building's height</s> <v>symbolizes</v> <o>the year of the United States' independence</o>. The glass-paneled tower has a twisted octagon shape, and its huge reflective surface mirrors the changes in light all around it. At the top is a spire equipped with a light beam that is visible for miles.

Several other high-rises are being constructed at the site as well. By the year 2020, the new World Trade Center complex should be complete. <s>New York's ever-changing skyline</s> <v>will reflect</v> <o>the spirit of a city</o> that continues to adapt and grow.

vocabulary.sentence: Soon skyscrapers were going up one after another as builders and architects competed to make the world's tallest building.

options: design buildings and create detailed plans for construction
drive trucks and deliver heavy loads across long distances
cook food and prepare meals in large restaurant kitchens
teach students and grade papers in a school classroom

vocabulary.definition: someone or something with immense size or power
vocabulary.sentence: Just blocks away, a steel-framed behemoth was rising.

options: very small and weak compared to most other things
extremely large and powerful in size or strength
old and broken beyond any useful purpose now
fast and colorful in a way that attracts attention

vocabulary.definition: a group of buildings close to one another used for a common purpose
vocabulary.sentence: He proposed twin towers as part of the multibuilding complex of offices and storefronts.

options: a single house built for one family to live in
a group of buildings used together for a common purpose
a type of bridge that connects two distant places together
an underground tunnel used for transportation between cities

vocabulary.definition: caused something to seem smaller or less important
vocabulary.sentence: Because they were built away from the city's other tall skyscrapers, they dwarfed the surrounding buildings.

options: destroyed completely by a powerful force or disaster
made something seem very small by comparison with something larger
painted in bright colors to attract attention from far away
surrounded by water on all sides like an island in the sea

vocabulary.definition: of or relating to an important symbol; famous and recognizable
vocabulary.sentence: Iconic structures such as these, along with surrounding buildings, create an outline against the sky, called a skyline.

options: famous and widely recognized as an important symbol of something
old and historical with connections to events from the distant past
tall and impressive in a way that catches everyone's attention
beautiful and colorful with many decorative features on display

vocabulary.definition: new ideas, products, or ways of doing something
vocabulary.sentence: The 110-story towers would include some structural innovations.

options: old traditions that have been followed for many generations
new ideas or creative ways of doing something differently
expensive materials used in the construction of large buildings
government laws that regulate how businesses must operate

vocabulary.definition: an important historical building or site; an object on land that marks a place
vocabulary.sentence: New Yorkers mourned the loss of their neighbors and their landmark.

options: a new building that is still being planned for the future
an important historical building or place that is widely recognized
a small house built for a family with limited space and resources
a park bench where people can sit and rest during the day

vocabulary.definition: having a complete view in every direction
vocabulary.sentence: An outside observation deck on the 86th floor and an inside deck on the 102nd floor provided panoramic views of the city.

options: narrow and limited in what can be seen from that vantage point
close-up and detailed, showing only a small area very clearly
providing a complete and wide view in all directions at once
dark and shadowy, making it difficult to see any clear details

vocabulary.definition: the measurement around the outside edge of an area or surface
vocabulary.sentence: The outside perimeter of the building had columns that were spaced close together.

options: the exact center point of something, like the middle of a room
the inside floor area of a building where people walk and stand
the outside edge or boundary that marks the limit of an area
the very top surface of something, such as the roof of a building

vocabulary.definition: easily seen, standing out, or important
vocabulary.sentence: They dwarfed the surrounding buildings and were the most prominent structures on Manhattan Island.

options: hidden from view and difficult for anyone to find or notice
very small in size and easy to overlook when walking past
easily seen and standing out as important among other things
old and forgotten after many years of being ignored by everyone

vocabulary.definition: a framework that supports a structure, such as a building
vocabulary.sentence: Gigantic steel beams were connected to form the building's skeleton.

options: the outer walls that enclose a building and keep weather out
a framework of steel beams that supports the entire structure
the roof that covers the top of a building and keeps rain out
the foundation buried underground that anchors a building in place

vocabulary.definition: in a way that is straight up and down
vocabulary.sentence: This system was a new, efficient way to transport people vertically, and it reduced the number of elevator shafts needed.

options: moving from side to side in a horizontal direction across a surface
moving in a circle around a fixed central point over and over
moving in a straight direction going up and down along a line
moving diagonally at an angle that is neither straight nor flat

sequenceEvents.event: The Park Row Building was constructed, standing 391 feet tall with 29 stories.
The Singer Building was completed, reaching 612 feet with 37 stories.
The Metropolitan Life Tower was built, towering 700 feet with 50 stories.
The Woolworth Building opened as the world's tallest at 792 feet with 55 stories.
The Chrysler Building became the world's tallest structure, surpassing 1,000 feet with 77 stories.
The Empire State Building was completed in just 410 days, standing 1,454 feet tall with 103 stories.
The World Trade Center North Tower was completed, reaching 1,727 feet with 110 stories.
One World Trade Center opened, rising 1,776 feet with 104 stories to symbolize American independence.

questions.question: How are city skylines similar to fingerprints? How are they different?
questions.evidence: Skylines are unique identifiers of places, just as your fingerprints are one-of-a-kind identifiers of you. Unlike fingerprints, however, skylines don't stay the same forever.
choices: Both serve as unique identifiers, but skylines change over time while fingerprints stay the same.
Both skylines and fingerprints stay exactly the same forever and never change at all.
Neither skylines nor fingerprints can serve as unique identifiers of anything.
Fingerprints change over time as people age, but skylines remain the same forever.
questions.explanation: The text says skylines are unique like fingerprints, BUT unlike fingerprints, skylines grow and change over time.

questions.question: What caused New York City to become the most densely populated city in the world by 1925?
questions.evidence: With the end of the Civil War came freed slaves from the South in search of work. Later, waves of Europeans immigrated to the United States through the port of New York. Many of them stayed on New York's Manhattan Island.
choices: People came from the American South and other countries to live in New York City.
The city built many taller buildings to fit more people on the small island.
The federal government forced people from other states to move to New York City.
New York City had the worst economy in the entire country at that time period.
questions.explanation: Freed slaves came from the South, and waves of Europeans immigrated through the port of New York, making it the most densely populated city.

questions.question: What kinds of structural innovations were used to build the World Trade Center towers?
questions.evidence: One innovation was the use of tube-frame construction instead of steel-frame construction. Another innovation was the use of sky lobbies.
choices: Tube-frame construction and sky lobbies with express and local elevators for efficiency.
Steel-frame construction and panoramic observation decks for tourists visiting the city.
Granite walls and underground parking garages built beneath both towers for vehicles.
Glass panels and reflective surfaces that mirrored the changes in light around them.
questions.explanation: The towers used tube-frame construction (like a hollow square pipe) and sky lobbies with express and local elevators, modeled after the subway system.

questions.question: How did the use of sky lobbies change New York's skyline?
questions.evidence: This system was a new, efficient way to transport people vertically, and it reduced the number of elevator shafts needed.
choices: They reduced the number of elevator shafts needed, making very tall buildings more practical to build.
They made buildings shorter and wider by reducing the number of floors that could be built.
They completely replaced the need for all stairs and stairways inside the new buildings.
They only served restaurants and dining areas on the very top floor of the buildings.
questions.explanation: Sky lobbies let people take express elevators partway, then transfer to local elevators. This reduced the number of elevator shafts, freeing up floor space and making taller buildings possible.

questions.question: How did the attacks on New York City on September 11, 2001, change the skyline?
questions.evidence: Within an hour after the attack, the South Tower began to fall in on itself and crumble to the ground. The North Tower followed thirty minutes later. New Yorkers watched in stunned disbelief as these enormous structures were reduced to heaps of burning rubble.
choices: The Twin Towers were destroyed and fell to the ground, dramatically changing the city's skyline.
New replacement towers were immediately built in their place within just a few short months.
Only small nearby buildings were damaged while the Twin Towers remained standing and intact.
The city's skyline stayed exactly the same because the towers were quickly repaired after the attack.
questions.explanation: Both Twin Towers collapsed on September 11, 2001, and were reduced to rubble. The skyline lost its most prominent structures.

questions.question: In what ways has the New York City skyline changed slowly? In what ways has it changed quickly?
questions.evidence: Gradually, the city expanded northward. Within an hour after the attack, the South Tower began to fall.
choices: Slowly: new skyscrapers rose over many decades. Quickly: the Twin Towers were destroyed on September 11.
Slowly: buildings were painted different colors each year. Quickly: old buildings were torn down overnight.
The New York City skyline only changed slowly over time and never changed quickly at all.
The New York City skyline only changed quickly due to disasters and never changed slowly at all.
questions.explanation: The skyline changed slowly as new buildings went up one after another over many decades. It changed quickly when the Twin Towers were destroyed within an hour on September 11, 2001.

questions.question: What would you change about New York City's skyline? Why?
questions.explanation: This is an opinion question. Support your answer with details from the book!
opinionPrompts: What would you add, remove, or change?
Why would you make this change?
What evidence from the book supports your idea?

summarizeActivities.mainIdea: New York City grew rapidly but had limited space, so it had to build upward using new steel-frame construction.
keyDetails: Freed slaves and European immigrants caused rapid population growth
Manhattan Island was only 23 square miles — no room to expand outward
Steel frame construction replaced wall-bearing construction
Steel skeleton supported the building's weight, allowing much taller buildings

summarizeActivities.mainIdea: In the late 1920s and early 1930s, two iconic skyscrapers were built that defined New York City's skyline.
keyDetails: The Chrysler Building became the world's tallest in 1930 with a secret spire
The Empire State Building was completed in just 410 days
The Empire State Building had 103 floors and stood over 1,400 feet tall
It was named one of the Seven Wonders of the Modern World
distractors: The Empire State Building was built in the 1970s

summarizeActivities.mainIdea: The World Trade Center's Twin Towers introduced new structural innovations but were destroyed on September 11, 2001.
keyDetails: Tube-frame construction created more usable floor space
Sky lobbies with express and local elevators were efficient
The Twin Towers were the tallest in New York for thirty years
On 9/11, both towers collapsed within an hour
distractors: The towers were built in the 1920s

summarizeActivities.sectionTitle: A New Tower Rises from the Ashes
summarizeActivities.mainIdea: After the 9/11 tragedy, New Yorkers rebuilt the World Trade Center site with a memorial and a new 1,776-foot tower.
keyDetails: Reflecting pools and waterfalls mark where the original towers stood
One World Trade Center rises 1,776 feet, symbolizing the year of U.S. independence
The tower has a twisted octagon shape with a glass surface
The new complex honors the past while looking to the future
distractors: The new tower is shorter than the original Twin Towers
No memorial was built at the site

intro.title: Iconic
intro.content: Means very famous and everyone recognizes it as a symbol. Like the Eiffel Tower is iconic for Paris!

intro.title: Rhetorical Question
intro.content: "What would Paris be without...?" - This is a rhetorical question. It doesn't need an answer! It's used to show something is very important.

intro.title: Unique
intro.content: Means one-of-a-kind, special, different from everything else. Your fingerprints are unique to you!

growing.title: Diverse
growing.content: Means having many different types. A diverse population means people from many different countries and backgrounds.

growing.title: Immigrated
growing.content: Means came to live in a new country permanently. Remember: IMMIGRATE = IN to a country.

growing.title: Simile
growing.content: "much like curtains from a rod" - This is a simile! It compares walls hanging from steel beams to curtains hanging from a rod. Uses "like" or "as".

growing.title: Skeleton
growing.content: Here means the framework that supports the building, like bones support your body!

empire.title: Reign
empire.content: Means the period when someone or something is the champion or in power. Like a king's reign!

empire.title: Broke Ground
empire.content: A phrase meaning to start construction. Workers break the ground to begin building!

empire.title: Panoramic
empire.content: Means a complete, wide view in all directions. From the top, you can see everything around you!

twins.title: Perimeter
twins.content: Means the outer edge or boundary. The perimeter of the building = the outside edge of the building.

twins.title: Dwarfed
twins.content: Means made something look very small by comparison. The tall towers dwarfed the small buildings!

twins.title: Prominent
twins.content: Means easily seen, standing out, or very important. The most prominent building is the one everyone notices!

twins.title: The + comparative, the + comparative
twins.content: "The taller a building is, the more elevators it needs" - This pattern shows that as one thing increases, another increases too!

twins.title: Stunned
twins.content: Means shocked, very surprised, often unable to react. Like when you see something unbelievable!

newtower.title: Landmark
newtower.content: An important or famous building or place that everyone knows. The Statue of Liberty is a landmark!

newtower.title: Symbolizes
newtower.content: Means represents or stands for something else. The height of 1,776 feet symbolizes the year 1776!

newtower.title: Adapt
newtower.content: Means to change or adjust to new conditions. New York adapts and grows, no matter what happens!
